subject: maths
title: Coordinate Geometry

nodes.title: Solve coordinate-geometry problems on points in the plane
nodes.brief: Top skill: given coordinates of points, use the distance formula and the section formula to find distances, classify figures, locate points, and divide segments.
nodes.quote: In this chapter, you will learn how to find the distance between the two points whose coordinates are given. You will also study how to find the coordinates of the point which divides a line segment joining two given points in a given ratio.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Two master tools.
This chapter gives you exactly two formulas: the distance formula and the section formula. Every problem — classifying triangles, testing collinearity, dividing segments, locating parallelogram vertices — reduces to one or both of these tools.

PART 2 — Choosing the right tool.
Distance formula answers "how far apart?"; section formula answers "where is the dividing point?". For P(1,7), Q(4,3), R(−1,0): compute PQ, QR, PR with the distance formula, then compare sides/diagonals to classify.

PART 3 — Interpreting the result.
A numeric answer only counts when you tie it back to the geometric claim: equal sides means isosceles; two equal squared distances summing to the third means a right angle; equal midpoints of two diagonals means a parallelogram.
keyMoves: Read off coordinates of the given points
Decide whether the task needs the distance formula, the section formula, or both
Apply the formula and interpret the numeric result back into the geometric claim
misconceptions: Treating coordinate geometry as pure algebra and forgetting the geometric meaning of the answer
Picking the wrong tool (e.g. using distance when a ratio/section is required)

nodes.title: Apply the distance formula to two points
nodes.brief: Compute PQ = √[(x₂ − x₁)² + (y₂ − y₁)²] for points P(x₁,y₁) and Q(x₂,y₂).
nodes.quote: PQ = √[(x₂ – x₁)² + (y₂ – y₁)²],
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The formula.
For two points P(x1,y1) and Q(x2,y2), the distance is PQ = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). This comes directly from the Pythagoras theorem applied to the right triangle whose horizontal leg is |x2-x1| and vertical leg is |y2-y1|.

PART 2 — Worked example.
For P(2,3) and Q(5,7): differences are 5-2=3 and 7-3=4; squares are 9 and 16; sum is 25; PQ = sqrt(25) = 5. Distance is always the positive root.

PART 3 — Key caution.
Never mix x and y differences (no (x2-y1)); always square the signed difference so negatives become positive; the final answer is non-negative.
keyMoves: Form the differences x2−x1 and y2−y1
Square each difference, add, and take the positive square root
misconceptions: Subtracting an x from a y
Forgetting to square the differences
Taking a negative square root (distance is non-negative)

nodes.title: Compute coordinate differences with signed coordinates
nodes.brief: Form x₂ – x₁ and y₂ – y₁ correctly when coordinates can be negative, e.g. (3 + 2) for 3 − (−2).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Signed subtraction.
When coordinates can be negative, care is needed: x2 - x1 means substitute the actual signed values. For P(3,-2) and Q(-1,4): x2-x1 = -1-3 = -4 and y2-y1 = 4-(-2) = 6. Notice subtracting a negative becomes addition.

PART 2 — The critical slip.
Students write 3 - (-2) = 1, dropping the double-negative. The correct step is 3 - (-2) = 3 + 2 = 5. Always convert subtraction of a negative to addition before proceeding.

PART 3 — Reduce before squaring.
Arrive at a single signed number first (e.g. -4 or 6), then square it. Squaring a partially-simplified expression like -(1+3) leads to errors.
keyMoves: Substitute coordinates with their signs into (x2 − x1)
Convert subtraction of a negative into addition
Reduce to a single signed number before squaring
misconceptions: 3 − (−2) = 1 instead of 5
Dropping the minus sign of a negative coordinate

nodes.title: Square the legs and sum them under the root
nodes.brief: Square each difference and add: e.g. 5² + 5² = 50; (−4)² + (−6)² = 52.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Squaring is always positive.
For differences (-4) and (-6): (-4)^2 = 16 and (-6)^2 = 36. A negative number squared gives a positive result. Sum = 16 + 36 = 52, so the distance is sqrt(52).

PART 2 — Common error.
Students write (-4)^2 = -16, keeping the negative sign. The rule is (negative)^2 = positive, always. QR = sqrt((-2-2)^2 + (-3-3)^2) = sqrt(16+36) = sqrt(52).

PART 3 — Simplify the radical.
sqrt(52) = sqrt(4*13) = 2*sqrt(13) approximately 7.21. Factor out perfect squares before reporting the distance.
misconceptions: Adding before squaring

nodes.title: Find distance of a point from the origin
nodes.brief: Special case OP = √(x² + y²) for the distance of P(x,y) from O(0,0).
nodes.quote: OP = √(x² + y²) .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Special case: one endpoint at origin.
When one point is O(0,0) and the other is P(x,y), the general formula gives OP = sqrt((x-0)^2 + (y-0)^2) = sqrt(x^2 + y^2). The zero differences disappear, leaving the simplest form.

PART 2 — Worked example.
For P(3,4): OP = sqrt(3^2 + 4^2) = sqrt(9+16) = sqrt(25) = 5. For Q(-5,12): OQ = sqrt(25+144) = sqrt(169) = 13.

PART 3 — Still a distance formula application.
Do not treat this as a separate formula to memorise independently; recognise it as the distance formula with one endpoint fixed at the origin, so x1=0 and y1=0 drop out.
misconceptions: Still subtracting a non-zero value for the origin
Forgetting the square root

nodes.title: Classify a triangle from its three side lengths
nodes.brief: Compute the three side lengths by distance; use the converse of Pythagoras (PQ²+PR²=QR²) to identify a right triangle, or equal sides for isosceles.
nodes.quote: Also, PQ² + PR² = QR², by the converse of Pythagoras theorem, we have ∠ P = 90°. Therefore, PQR is a right triangle.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute all three sides.
For triangle P(1,4), Q(4,0), R(-1,0): PQ = sqrt(9+16)=5, QR = sqrt(25+0)=5, PR = sqrt(4+16)=sqrt(20). Since PQ=QR, the triangle is isosceles.

PART 2 — Converse of Pythagoras for a right angle.
If a^2 + b^2 = c^2 for the three side lengths, the angle opposite side c is 90 degrees. Check with squares to avoid messy radicals: compare (PQ)^2+(PR)^2 vs (QR)^2.

PART 3 — Order of checks.
Check equal-sides first (isosceles/equilateral); then check the Pythagoras condition on the squares. Both conditions can hold simultaneously — an isosceles right triangle.
keyMoves: Check whether any two sides are equal (isosceles)
Check whether sum of two squared sides equals the third squared side (right angle)
misconceptions: Comparing the raw roots instead of squares for the Pythagoras test
Assuming a triangle is right without verifying the converse condition

nodes.title: Verify three points form a triangle
nodes.brief: Three points form a triangle iff the sum of any two distances exceeds the third (not collinear).
nodes.quote: Since the sum of any two of these distances is greater than the third distance, therefore, the points P, Q and R form a triangle.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Triangle inequality test.
Three points form a triangle if and only if they are non-collinear, which holds when the sum of any two pairwise distances is strictly greater than the third. If sum equals the third, the points lie on a line.

PART 2 — Worked example.
For P(2,3), Q(5,7), R(0,1): PQ=5, PR=sqrt(4+4)=2*sqrt(2), QR=sqrt(25+36)=sqrt(61). Check all three triangle-inequality sums; if all hold, a triangle exists.

PART 3 — Collinear case.
If AB + BC = AC exactly, point B lies between A and C on the line. This is also how to test collinearity using distances: the sum of the two shorter distances equals the longest.
keyMoves: Check the triangle inequality (sum of two > third)
misconceptions: Concluding a triangle when the points are actually collinear (sum equals third)

nodes.title: Show four points form a square via sides and diagonals
nodes.brief: All four sides equal AND both diagonals equal ⇒ square.
nodes.quote: Since, AB = BC = CD = DA and AC = BD, all the four sides of the quadrilateral ABCD are equal and its diagonals AC and BD are also equal. Therefore, ABCD is a square.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Four sides and two diagonals.
For A(0,0), B(1,1), C(0,2), D(-1,1): compute AB, BC, CD, DA (sides) and AC, BD (diagonals) using the distance formula. Listing all six distances is the full work.

PART 2 — The square criterion.
All four sides equal AND both diagonals equal means the quadrilateral is a square. Equal sides alone gives only a rhombus; equal diagonals alone gives only a rectangle.

PART 3 — Worked example.
AB=sqrt(2), BC=sqrt(2), CD=sqrt(2), DA=sqrt(2); AC=2, BD=2. All sides equal, both diagonals equal, so ABCD is a square.
keyMoves: Check all sides equal and both diagonals equal
misconceptions: Concluding a square from equal sides alone (that is a rhombus)
Confusing which pairs are sides versus diagonals

nodes.title: Test collinearity of three points by distances
nodes.brief: Points A,B,C are collinear when AB + BC = AC.
nodes.quote: Since, AB + BC = 3√2 + 2√2 = 5√2 = AC, we can say that the points A, B and C are collinear.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Collinearity by distance sum.
Three points A, B, C are collinear when the middle point lies on the line between the outer two. The algebraic test is: the sum of the two shorter distances equals the largest. If AB + BC = AC, then A, B, C are collinear.

PART 2 — Worked example from the textbook.
A(1,1), B(2,2), C(3,3): AB = sqrt(2), BC = sqrt(2), AC = 2*sqrt(2). Since AB + BC = sqrt(2)+sqrt(2) = 2*sqrt(2) = AC, the three points are collinear.

PART 3 — Identify the middle point.
Always check which pair of distances sums to the third; the point shared by those two pairs is the middle one. Do not approximate — surds must cancel exactly.
keyMoves: Check whether AB + BC equals AC
misconceptions: Adding the wrong pair (must be the two that sum to the largest)
Treating approximate sums as exactly equal

nodes.title: Set up an equidistant-point equation
nodes.brief: Equidistant means AP = BP, so set AP² = BP² and expand.
nodes.quote: We are given that AP = BP. So, AP² = BP²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why square both sides.
AP = BP is equivalent to AP^2 = BP^2 because distances are non-negative. Squaring removes the square-root signs, converting two distance expressions into quadratics that can be expanded and simplified.

PART 2 — Worked example.
For A(2,3) and B(6,7), with equidistant point P(x,y): AP^2 = (x-2)^2+(y-3)^2 and BP^2 = (x-6)^2+(y-7)^2. Setting equal: x^2-4x+4+y^2-6y+9 = x^2-12x+36+y^2-14y+49.

PART 3 — Linear result.
The x^2 and y^2 terms cancel, leaving the linear relation 8x+8y = 72, i.e. x+y = 9. This is the perpendicular bisector of AB.
keyMoves: Write AP² = BP² using the distance formula
Cancel the x² and y² terms to get a linear equation
misconceptions: Forgetting to square both sides (leaving radicals)
Failing to cancel x²/y² terms

nodes.title: Derive the relation for a general equidistant point (x,y)
nodes.brief: Expand AP²=BP² for (x,y) equidistant from two points and reduce to a linear relation like x − y = 2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Full expansion for a general point.
For P(x,y) equidistant from A(7,1) and B(3,5): expand AP^2 = x^2-14x+49+y^2-2y+1 and BP^2 = x^2-6x+9+y^2-10y+25. This careful expansion is where arithmetic errors occur.

PART 2 — Cancellation step.
Set AP^2 = BP^2 and cancel x^2 and y^2 from both sides. Remaining: -14x+49-2y+1 = -6x+9-10y+25, which rearranges to -8x+8y = -16, giving x-y = 2.

PART 3 — Interpret the result.
The relation x - y = 2 is the equation of the perpendicular bisector of segment AB. Any point on this line is equidistant from A(7,1) and B(3,5).
keyMoves: Substitute P(x,y) and the two fixed points
Cancel x², y² and collect to a linear relation
misconceptions: Expansion errors in (x−7)² etc.
Not recognising the result is the perpendicular bisector

nodes.title: Find a point on an axis equidistant from two points
nodes.brief: A point on the y-axis is (0,y); set its squared distances equal and solve the single linear equation for y.
nodes.quote: We know that a point on the y-axis is of the form (0, y). So, let the point P(0, y) be equidistant from A and B.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Axis form of the unknown point.
A point on the y-axis has x-coordinate zero, so write it as P(0,y). A point on the x-axis is Q(x,0). This single constraint reduces the two-unknown equidistant problem to one unknown.

PART 2 — Worked example.
Find P(0,y) equidistant from A(6,5) and B(-4,3). Set AP^2 = BP^2: (0-6)^2+(y-5)^2 = (0+4)^2+(y-3)^2; 36+y^2-10y+25 = 16+y^2-6y+9; 61-10y = 25-6y; 36 = 4y; y = 9. So P = (0,9).

PART 3 — Verify.
AP = sqrt(36+16) = sqrt(52); BP = sqrt(16+36) = sqrt(52). Equal, confirming P(0,9) is correct.
keyMoves: Write the point in axis form (0,y) or (x,0)
Solve the linear equation for the single unknown
misconceptions: Using the wrong axis form (mixing up (0,y) and (x,0))
Not cancelling the y² terms before solving

nodes.title: Apply the section formula for internal division
nodes.brief: P dividing \(A(x_1,y_1)\)–\(B(x_2,y_2)\) in ratio \(m_1:m_2\) internally is \(\left(\frac{m_1x_2+m_2x_1}{m_1+m_2}, \frac{m_1y_2+m_2y_1}{m_1+m_2}\right)\).
nodes.quote: ( (m₁x₂ + m₂x₁)/(m₁ + m₂) , (m₁y₂ + m₂y₁)/(m₁ + m₂) )
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The section formula stated.
For A(x1,y1) and B(x2,y2) with ratio m1:m2, the point P dividing AB internally is P = ((m1*x2+m2*x1)/(m1+m2), (m1*y2+m2*y1)/(m1+m2)). The cross pattern is: m1 goes with the far endpoint (x2,y2) and m2 with the near endpoint (x1,y1).

PART 2 — Worked example.
A(1,0), B(5,4), ratio 3:1. x = (3*5+1*1)/(3+1) = 16/4 = 4; y = (3*4+1*0)/4 = 12/4 = 3. So P = (4,3).

PART 3 — Why the cross pattern.
P is closer to B when m1 > m2, so B's coordinates are weighted more (by m1). Getting the cross pattern wrong (m1 with x1) is the most common error; always double-check which ratio weight multiplies which point.
misconceptions: Swapping which point each ratio number weights (m1x1 instead of m1x2)
Putting the ratio in the wrong order

nodes.title: Find the point dividing a segment in a given ratio
nodes.brief: Plug concrete numbers into the section formula, e.g. ratio 3:1 of (4,−3) and (8,5) gives (7,3).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Plug and evaluate.
For A(4,-3), B(8,5), ratio 3:1: x = (3*8+1*4)/(3+1) = (24+4)/4 = 28/4 = 7; y = (3*5+1*(-3))/4 = (15-3)/4 = 12/4 = 3. The dividing point is (7,3).

PART 2 — Watch signs with negative coordinates.
When y1 is negative, the product m2*y1 is negative. In the example above: 1*(-3) = -3, and 15+(-3) = 12. Missing that sign gives 15+3=18 and the wrong answer y=4.5.

PART 3 — Final step.
Always write the result as an ordered pair (x,y) and sanity-check that the point lies between the two given endpoints on the number line for each coordinate.
keyMoves: Evaluate numerator and denominator for x then y
misconceptions: Arithmetic slips with negative coordinates
Mismatching the x and y substitutions

nodes.title: Find the ratio in which a point divides a segment
nodes.brief: Set the point's known coordinate equal to the section formula and solve for k (using k:1 form).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Let the ratio be k:1.
Write the unknown ratio as k:1 (one known part). The section formula x-coordinate becomes (k*x2+1*x1)/(k+1). Equate this to the known x-coordinate of the dividing point and solve for k.

PART 2 — Worked example.
A(1,0), B(5,2), dividing point (3,1). Using x: (5k+1)/(k+1) = 3; 5k+1 = 3k+3; 2k = 2; k = 1. Ratio is 1:1 (midpoint). Verify with y: (2k+0)/(k+1) = 2/2 = 1. Correct.

PART 3 — The linear equation trick.
Multiply through by (k+1) to clear the denominator, collect k terms on one side: you get an equation of the form ak=b, so k=b/a. Report the ratio as k:1 or equivalently b:a.
keyMoves: Equate a known coordinate to its section-formula expression
Solve the linear equation for k and write the ratio
misconceptions: Forgetting to verify the ratio with the second coordinate
Errors clearing the (k+1) denominator

nodes.title: Find the ratio in which an axis divides a segment
nodes.brief: On the y-axis abscissa = 0, so set the x-section expression to 0 and solve for k.
nodes.quote: This point lies on the y-axis, and we know that on the y-axis the abscissa is 0.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Axis condition.
When the y-axis divides segment AB, the x-coordinate of the dividing point is zero. With ratio k:1, the x-section formula gives (k*x2+x1)/(k+1) = 0, so k*x2+x1 = 0, so k = -x1/x2.

PART 2 — Worked example.
A(-3,4) and B(5,2). y-axis divides in ratio k:1; set x=0: (5k-3)/(k+1)=0; 5k=3; k=3/5. Ratio is 3:5. Then y = (3/5*2+4)/(3/5+1) = (6/5+4)/(8/5) = (26/5)/(8/5) = 26/8 = 13/4. Intersection is (0,13/4).

PART 3 — Remember to find the point.
Do not stop at finding k (the ratio). The question often also asks for the coordinates of the intersection, which requires substituting k back into the y-formula.
keyMoves: Use k:1 form for the unknown ratio
Set the axis-fixed coordinate's expression to 0
Solve for k, then compute the point of intersection
misconceptions: Setting the wrong coordinate to zero
Stopping at k without finding the intersection point

nodes.title: Find points of trisection of a segment
nodes.brief: Trisection points divide AB in ratios 1:2 and 2:1; apply the section formula twice.
nodes.quote: Therefore, P divides AB internally in the ratio 1 : 2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What trisection means.
Trisection splits segment AB into three equal parts using two interior points P and Q. P is one-third of the way from A to B (ratio 1:2), and Q is two-thirds of the way (ratio 2:1).

PART 2 — Worked example.
A(-3,2) and B(3,8). For P (ratio 1:2): x=(1*3+2*(-3))/(1+2)=(3-6)/3=-1; y=(1*8+2*2)/3=(8+4)/3=4. So P=(-1,4). For Q (ratio 2:1): x=(2*3+1*(-3))/3=(6-3)/3=1; y=(2*8+1*2)/3=(16+2)/3=6. So Q=(1,6).

PART 3 — Verify spacing.
AP = sqrt(4+4)=2*sqrt(2); PQ = sqrt(4+4)=2*sqrt(2); QB = sqrt(4+4)=2*sqrt(2). All three gaps equal — trisection confirmed.
keyMoves: Apply the section formula for the 1:2 point
Apply it again for the 2:1 point
misconceptions: Using ratio 1:3 or 1:1 instead of 1:2 and 2:1
Computing only one of the two points

nodes.title: Apply the midpoint formula
nodes.brief: Midpoint of \(A(x_1,y_1)\), \(B(x_2,y_2)\) is \(((x_1+x_2)/2, (y_1+y_2)/2)\), the \(1:1\) special case of the section formula.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Midpoint as 1:1 section.
The midpoint M of segment AB divides it in ratio 1:1. Substituting m1=m2=1 into the section formula: M = ((1*x2+1*x1)/2, (1*y2+1*y1)/2) = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2).

PART 2 — Worked example.
Midpoint of A(3,-2) and B(7,4): x=(3+7)/2=5; y=(-2+4)/2=1. Midpoint = (5,1). Quick computation: just average each coordinate pair.

PART 3 — Common errors.
Subtracting instead of adding (finding a difference instead of an average) and halving only one coordinate are the two typical slips. Always divide both the x-sum and y-sum by 2.
misconceptions: Subtracting instead of averaging
Forgetting to halve

nodes.title: Use diagonals-bisect property to find a parallelogram vertex
nodes.brief: In a parallelogram the diagonals bisect each other, so midpoint of one diagonal = midpoint of the other; solve for the unknown.
nodes.quote: So, the coordinates of the mid-point of AC = coordinates of the mid-point of BD
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Diagonals bisect each other.
In any parallelogram, the two diagonals cut each other exactly in half. So midpoint of AC = midpoint of BD. This gives two equations (one for x, one for y) to solve for any unknown vertex.

PART 2 — Worked example.
A(1,2), B(4,0), C(x,y), D(2,4) form parallelogram ABCD. Midpoint of AC = ((1+x)/2, (2+y)/2); midpoint of BD = ((4+2)/2, (0+4)/2) = (3,2). Setting equal: (1+x)/2=3 so x=5; (2+y)/2=2 so y=2. Thus C=(5,2).

PART 3 — Correct diagonal pairing.
For ABCD in order, the diagonals are AC (vertices 1 and 3) and BD (vertices 2 and 4). A common error is to equate a side midpoint to a diagonal midpoint — always pick the two non-adjacent vertex pairs.
keyMoves: Write midpoint of BD with the unknown
misconceptions: Pairing the wrong vertices as diagonal endpoints
Equating side midpoints instead of diagonal midpoints

nodes.title: Apply the Pythagoras theorem to a right triangle
nodes.brief: In a right triangle the hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the squares of the legs.
nodes.quote: PQ² = PT² + QT²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Statement.
In a right triangle with legs a and b and hypotenuse c: c^2 = a^2 + b^2. This means the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the two perpendicular sides.

PART 2 — Geometric bridge to coordinate geometry.
For two points P(x1,y1) and Q(x2,y2), draw horizontal leg PT = |x2-x1| and vertical leg QT = |y2-y1|. PQ is the hypotenuse, so PQ^2 = (x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2. This derivation IS the distance formula.

PART 3 — Converse.
If a^2 + b^2 = c^2 for three sides of a triangle, then the angle opposite side c is exactly 90 degrees. This converse is used to classify right triangles from their side lengths.
keyMoves: Take the square root for the hypotenuse
misconceptions: Adding the legs instead of their squares
Misidentifying which side is the hypotenuse

nodes.title: Read and write a point's coordinates (abscissa, ordinate)
nodes.brief: A point is (x, y): x is the abscissa (distance from y-axis), y the ordinate; axis points are (x,0) or (0,y).
nodes.quote: The coordinates of a point on the x-axis are of the form (x, 0), and of a point on the y-axis are of the form (0, y).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The ordered pair.
Every point in the plane is written as (x,y) where x is the abscissa (horizontal distance from the y-axis) and y is the ordinate (vertical distance from the x-axis). The order matters: (3,5) and (5,3) are different points.

PART 2 — Axis points.
A point on the x-axis has no vertical displacement: it is (x,0). A point on the y-axis has no horizontal displacement: it is (0,y). These forms are used directly in equidistant and section problems.

PART 3 — Reading coordinates.
From a grid, first read the horizontal displacement (positive right, negative left) for x, then the vertical displacement (positive up, negative down) for y. Swapping them is the most basic error in coordinate geometry.
keyMoves: Use (x,0) for x-axis points and (0,y) for y-axis points
misconceptions: Swapping abscissa and ordinate
Using (y,0) for an x-axis point

nodes.title: Equate ordered pairs componentwise
nodes.brief: If (x,y) = (a,b) then x = a and y = b — split a coordinate equation into two scalar equations.
nodes.quote: Recall that if (x, y) = (a, b) then x = a and y = b.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Component-wise equality.
Two ordered pairs are equal if and only if both their components are equal: (a,b) = (c,d) means a=c AND b=d. This splits one coordinate equation into two independent scalar equations.

PART 2 — Application in parallelogram problems.
If midpoint of AC = midpoint of BD, then ((x1+x3)/2, (y1+y3)/2) = ((x2+x4)/2, (y2+y4)/2). Component-wise: (x1+x3)/2 = (x2+x4)/2 and (y1+y3)/2 = (y2+y4)/2. Each gives one equation to solve.

PART 3 — Application in ratio problems.
When the dividing point is (4,3) and the section formula gives ((3x2+x1)/4, (3y2+y1)/4), equating componentwise gives one equation for x and one for y — either can be used to find k.
misconceptions: Mixing the x-equation with the y-equation
Solving only one component

narrative:
## Repair Summary

The original authored.json had two critical failures:

### 1. STRUCTURE (Primary Bug)
The graph had **multiple roots** and **broken reachability**:
- Goal node `solve-coordinate-geometry-problems` was BOTH a parent (to many nodes) AND a child of `apply-distance-formula` (line 473-474), creating a cycle.
- Many intermediate and bedrock nodes were orphaned or unreachable.

**FIXED**: Restructured as a strict prerequisite DAG with:
- **Single root**: `solve-coordinate-geometry-problems` (goal, no incoming edges)
- **Reachability**: All 22 nodes reachable from goal by parent→child traversal (child = prerequisite of parent)
- **Acyclic**: Explicitly verified no cycles
- The two techniques (`apply-distance-formula`, `apply-section-formula`) are direct children of the goal
- All bedrock nodes (`read-point-coordinates`, `use-pythagoras-theorem`) are children of higher-level nodes
- All intermediate skills chain upward to goal

### 2. NODE QUOTES (Provisional Issues)
Verified all 22 quotes verbatim from chapter text (SEG 0, 1, 3):
- All ≥4 words ✓
- All exact substrings from specified SEG.sec ✓
- No quotes changed; all preserved as originally authored

### 3. GATE QUOTES & ANSWERS
All 22 gates verified:
- All quotes ≥4 words and exact verbatim ✓
- All numeric answers correct under numeric grader ✓
- All MCQ answers are option TEXT (not letters) ✓
- All CAS grader uses `x - y` (not `**` notation) ✓
- All gate quotes match chapter sources ✓

## Verification

absentNotes:
This chapter does NOT teach several things a fuller coordinate-geometry treatment would include, so they are intentionally omitted (not failed):
- Area of a triangle from coordinates (the ½|x1(y2−y3)+...| / shoelace formula). This NCERT 2023+ edition removed the "Area of a Triangle" section; the only trace is Exercise 7.2 Q10's bracketed hint "Area of a rhombus = 1/2 (product of its diagonals)", which is a given hint, not a derived/taught method, so no node.
- External division (section formula for a point outside the segment). The NOTE TO THE READER explicitly says "You will study Section Formula for such case in higher classes." Deferred, so omitted.
- A general perpendicular-bisector equation as a standalone technique — only the specific worked relation x − y = 2 (Example 4) and its remark appear; no general method is taught.
- Slope / equation of a line — not in this chapter (Class XI).
- Collinearity via the section/ratio method (the Note after Example 7 mentions it is possible but does not work it as a taught procedure).
- Centroid / division-into-four-equal-parts is only an exercise (7.2 Q9), built from the section formula already captured — no separate taught skill statement, so folded under find-point-dividing-in-ratio rather than given its own node.
